lines: @@ -862,7 +862,7 @@ The sha1 HMAC fallback for API key authentication has been removed. API keys are
The MCP plugin was refactored. The public config API, the built-in tool inputs, and the API key collection schema all changed. Update your plugin config and delete + recreate any existing API keys.

-**Collections and globals are now opt-out.** Drop the `enabled` flag. Every collection and global is exposed by default with the standard CRUD tools (find, create, update, delete); turn individual ones off via `tools: { <op>: false }`.

+**Collections and globals are now opt-out.** Drop the `enabled` flag. Every collection and global is exposed by default through the generic built-in tools (`getConfigInfo`, `getCollectionSchema`, `findDocuments`, `createDocument`, `updateDocument`, `deleteDocuments`, plus `getGlobalSchema`, `findGlobal`, and `updateGlobal` for globals). Turn individual operations off via simple config keys like `tools: { create: false }`.
Prompts and resources move the same way, into top-level `prompts` and `resources` records.

-**Built-in `create` / `update` tool inputs now take a `data` object.** Document fields used to sit at the top level alongside options like `depth`, `draft` and `locale`. They are now nested under `data`. `_status`, `id`, `createdAt` and `updatedAt` are also no longer accepted there; set publish state with a custom tool if you need to.

+For custom collection and global tools, do not add `collectionSlug` / `globalSlug` yourself. Payload adds the slug input to the MCP schema automatically, and the handler receives the resolved slug as a top-level argument.
+  description: 'Publish a draft post by ID.',
+  // collectionSlug is resolved from the tool call

+**Built-in collection tools are now generic.** Per-collection names like `createPosts` / `updatePosts` were replaced by `createDocument` / `updateDocument`. Pass the target collection as `collectionSlug`; document fields live under `data` alongside options like `depth`, `draft` and `locale`. `_status`, `id`, `createdAt` and `updatedAt` are no longer accepted inside `data`; set publish state with a custom tool if you need to.
+{ collectionSlug: 'posts', data: { title: 'Hello' }, draft: true, depth: 2 }

-**Auth tools are per-collection and opt-in.** The old `experimental.tools.auth` block is gone. Opt in per collection via `tools: { login: true }`, and the wire name becomes `login<Collection>` (e.g. `loginUsers`).

+**Auth tools are per-collection and opt-in.** The old `experimental.tools.auth` block is gone. Opt in per collection via `tools: { login: true }`. Auth tool names are generic (`login`, `auth`, etc.); pass the target auth collection as `collectionSlug`.
- mcpPlugin({ experimental: { tools: { auth: { enabled: true } } } })
- Two transports out of the box:
- **HTTP** at `/api/mcp` - for remote clients, protected by per-user API keys
- **stdio** via the `payload-mcp` bin - for local AI clients spawning a child process
-- Every collection and global automatically gets `find`, `create`, `update`, `delete` (and `find` / `update` for globals) tools - opt out per tool with `tools: { delete: false }`

+- Stable discovery and CRUD tools for all collections and globals (`getConfigInfo`, `getCollectionSchema`, `findDocuments`, `createDocument`, `updateDocument`, `deleteDocuments`, `getGlobalSchema`, `findGlobal`, `updateGlobal`) - opt out per operation with simple keys like `tools: { delete: false }`
- Define your own `tools`, `prompts`, and `resources` with full TypeScript inference via `defineTool` / `defineCollectionTool` / `defineGlobalTool` / `definePrompt`
- Per-API-key access control through an admin-UI managed collection - toggle any tool, prompt, or resource on/off per key in real time
-That's it - every collection and global is now available as MCP tools (`findPosts`,

+That's it - every collection and global is now available through the generic MCP tools. For example, call `getConfigInfo` to see available slugs, inspect a collection with `getCollectionSchema`, then create a document with `createDocument`.
The `payload-mcp` bin finds your `payload.config.ts` automatically, since it's installed
inside your project. If your project has an unusual layout (e.g. a monorepo), see
-        'Published blog articles. Use `find` to list or fetch one by ID.',
+        'Published blog articles. Use `findDocuments` to list or fetch one by ID.',
// Built-in opt-out: keep find/create/update, block delete
-        // Built-in override: keep find enabled, just refine its description
+        // Built-in override: keep findDocuments enabled, just refine its description
'Find blog posts. Pass an `id` to fetch one; omit it to list with pagination.',

-| `description`      | `string`   | Description shown to MCP clients for every tool on this collection (unless a specific tool overrides it).                                                               |

+| `description`      | `string`   | Description shown for this collection in discovery responses.                                                                                                          |

| `tools`            | `object`   | Map of tool name → configuration. See [Configuring tools](#configuring-per-collection-tools) and [Defining custom tools](#defining-custom-tools-prompts-and-resources). |

| `overrideResponse` | `function` | Intercept the response of any built-in tool for this collection (`(response, doc, req) => response`). See [Modifying responses](#modifying-responses).                  |

-- A built-in name (`find`, `create`, `update`, `delete`, plus auth tool names on auth collections): set to `false` to disable, or to an override object `{ description?, overrideResponse? }`. Auth tools also accept `true` to enable with no overrides.

+- A built-in key (`getCollectionSchema`, `find`, `create`, `update`, `delete`, plus auth tool names on auth collections): set to `false` to disable, or to an override object `{ description?, overrideResponse? }`. Auth tools also accept `true` to enable with no overrides.
- A custom name: a value produced by `defineCollectionTool(...)`.
+`getConfigInfo` is always registered as a top-level tool. It returns the collection and global slugs visible to the current MCP client. API keys can disable it like any other top-level tool.
-`globals[slug]` accepts the same shape, but the built-in keys are limited to `find` and `update` (globals are singletons; create / delete don't apply).
+`globals[slug]` accepts the same shape, but the built-in keys are limited to `getGlobalSchema`, `find`, and `update` (globals are singletons; create / delete don't apply).
-A collection tool's wire name is `<key><PascalSlug>` (e.g. `publishPosts`). The handler additionally receives `collectionSlug`.

+A collection tool's wire name is exactly its key. Payload adds a `collectionSlug` string to the MCP input schema automatically; the handler receives the resolved `collectionSlug`. The field is intentionally not enum-restricted so newly-added collections can be used after HMR even if the MCP client cached the previous tool schema.
description: 'Publish a draft post by ID.',

-If you need to derive a Zod / JSON Schema from the collection itself, `input` can be a function that receives the JSON Schema for the collection - useful for "passthrough" tools that accept a partial document of any shape:

+`defineGlobalTool` mirrors `defineCollectionTool`. Payload adds a `globalSlug` string to the MCP input schema automatically; the handler receives the resolved `globalSlug`. The field is intentionally not enum-restricted so newly-added globals can be used after HMR even if the MCP client cached the previous tool schema.
-  description: 'Bulk-import posts from a JSON payload.',
-`defineGlobalTool` mirrors `defineCollectionTool`, with the handler receiving `globalSlug` instead of `collectionSlug`.
@@ -614,7 +628,7 @@ Built-in CRUD tools already use this pattern, so they automatically respect the
-The built-in `find` tool for both collections and globals accepts an optional `select` parameter. It limits which fields come back, which significantly reduces token usage on large documents.

+The built-in `findDocuments`, `findGlobal`, `createDocument`, `updateDocument`, and `updateGlobal` tools accept an optional `select` parameter. It limits which fields come back, which significantly reduces token usage on large documents.
`select` is a JSON string following [Payload's Select API](https://payloadcms.com/docs/queries/select) syntax - set a field to `true` to include it:

-Virtual fields (computed, read-only fields) are automatically excluded from the `create` and `update` tool schemas. They cannot be set by a model, so including them in the schema would be misleading and add noise. They are still present in `find` responses.
-The same stripping applies to the `collectionSchema` / `globalSchema` argument passed to function-form `input` in custom tools.

+Virtual fields (computed, read-only fields) are automatically excluded from `getCollectionSchema` and `getGlobalSchema`. They cannot be set by a model, so including them in the schema would be misleading and add noise. They are still present in find responses.
@@ -752,7 +766,7 @@ There are several levers for reducing the number of tokens consumed per MCP requ

-The description you provide for a collection or global is the primary signal a model uses to decide which tool to call. A vague description leads to missed or incorrect tool calls; a precise description gets the right tool called on the first try.

+The description you provide for a collection or global helps the model choose the right `collectionSlug` / `globalSlug` after inspecting the available schemas. A vague description leads to missed or incorrect tool calls; a precise description gets the right document target on the first try.
// Weak - the model has no idea what kind of posts these are
@@ -783,7 +797,7 @@ If a collection has fields that are irrelevant or sensitive for the model's task
### Only enable the operations you need

-If a model only needs to read data, disable `create`, `update`, and `delete`. Every additional tool adds more entries to `tools/list`, which costs tokens and increases the chance of an unintended action.
+If a model only needs to read data, disable `create`, `update`, and `delete`.
